region.name: Northland / Te Tai Tokerau
region.description: The long warm peninsula where Aotearoa begins — kauri forest, the Treaty ground, Cape Reinga's leaping place of spirits, and the Bay of Islands.

pois.name: Cape Reinga / Te Rerenga Wairua
pois.short: The leaping place of spirits — where the Tasman Sea meets the Pacific and Māori tradition says souls begin their journey home.
pois.commentary: Te Rerenga Wairua is one of the most sacred sites in Aotearoa. In Māori tradition this is where the spirits of the dead leave the land, climbing down the roots of an 800-year-old pōhutukawa tree to begin their journey back to Hawaiki. The 1941 lighthouse sits 165 m above the sea. Below, you can watch the Tasman and the Pacific visibly collide — two currents, two colours, one churn. Please: no food, no picnics on the headland. It is a tapu place. The walk from the carpark is short and easy.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Cape Reinga / Te Rerenga Wairua as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The leaping place of spirits — where the Tasman Sea meets the Pacific and Māori tradition says souls begin their journey home. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Waitangi Treaty Grounds
pois.short: Where Te Tiriti o Waitangi was signed on 6 February 1840 — New Zealand's founding document.
pois.commentary: On 6 February 1840, representatives of the Crown and around 45 Māori rangatira signed Te Tiriti o Waitangi on the lawn in front of James Busby's house. Over the following months a further ~500 chiefs signed copies around the country. The English and Māori texts differ significantly — a discrepancy still being worked through in law and politics today. The site now includes Busby's Residence, the carved Whare Rūnanga opened in 1940 (representing all iwi), the 35-metre ceremonial waka *Ngātokimatawhaorua*, and the Museum of Waitangi. Budget half a day.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Waitangi Treaty Grounds as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Where Te Tiriti o Waitangi was signed on 6 February 1840 — New Zealand's founding document. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Kerikeri Mission Station
pois.short: Kemp House (1821–22) is NZ's oldest surviving European building. The Stone Store next door is the oldest stone building.
pois.commentary: Samuel Marsden established a Church Missionary Society station here in 1819 under the protection of Ngāpuhi chief Hongi Hika. Kemp House, a two-storey Georgian weatherboard built 1821–22, is the oldest European building still standing in New Zealand. The Stone Store beside it (1832–36) is the oldest stone building, and still operates as a shop. Across the river is Kororipo Pā — Hongi Hika's base, and the launching point of the early-1820s Musket Wars that reshaped the North Island. A 20-minute walk links the three sites.
sources_to_check: Heritage NZ — Kemp House, Stone Store

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Kerikeri Mission Station — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. Kemp House (1821–22) is NZ's oldest surviving European building. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Russell / Kororāreka
pois.short: NZ's first European settlement and briefly its capital — once called 'the Hell-hole of the Pacific.'
pois.commentary: In the 1830s Kororāreka was the busiest port in New Zealand: whaling crews, runaway sailors, grog shops, prostitution. Charles Darwin passed through in 1835 and was unimpressed. Briefly the colony's capital (1840–41), it was sacked by Hōne Heke and Kawiti in March 1845 — the opening battle of the Northern War. Heke had felled the flagstaff on Maiki Hill four times. Christ Church (1836) still bears musket holes from that engagement. Today Russell is quiet, small, and historically astonishing. Walk the waterfront; visit Pompallier House (1842, rammed earth).

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Russell / Kororāreka — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. NZ's first European settlement and briefly its capital — once called 'the Hell-hole of the Pacific. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Waipoua Forest / Tāne Mahuta
pois.short: Tāne Mahuta — 'Lord of the Forest' — the largest living kauri, 51 m tall, ~2,000 years old.
pois.commentary: Tāne Mahuta stands in Waipoua Forest, the largest remaining tract of native kauri in the country. The tree is around 51 metres tall, nearly 14 metres around at the trunk, and estimated at 1,250–2,500 years old. Iwi Te Roroa are kaitiaki. A five-minute boardwalk from SH12 takes you to the viewing platform. Before you enter: stop at the cleaning station and spray/scrub your shoes. Kauri dieback disease (*Phytophthora agathidicida*) is spread by soil on footwear and is killing the species. Cleaning is not optional.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Waipoua Forest / Tāne Mahuta as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Tāne Mahuta — 'Lord of the Forest' — the largest living kauri, 51 m tall, ~2,000 years old. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Hokianga Harbour / Ōpononi
pois.short: The 'place of returning' — where the explorer Kupe is said to have left Aotearoa to sail back to Hawaiki.
pois.commentary: The Hokianga is one of the origin places of Aotearoa. Tradition says the Polynesian navigator Kupe landed here, explored the country, and departed from this harbour — hence Te Hokianga-nui-a-Kupe, 'the great returning place of Kupe.' The huge bright sand dunes on the north head face the tiny town of Ōpononi across the water. In 1955–56 a wild dolphin called Opo played with swimmers in the shallows and briefly made Ōpononi the most visited place in New Zealand. There's a statue on the foreshore. The Rawene car ferry crosses the narrow middle of the harbour on SH12.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Hokianga Harbour / Ōpononi as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The 'place of returning' — where the explorer Kupe is said to have left Aotearoa to sail back to Hawaiki. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Kawakawa Hundertwasser Toilets
pois.short: The most photographed public toilets in New Zealand — designed by Austrian artist Friedensreich Hundertwasser, 1999.
pois.commentary: Friedensreich Hundertwasser moved to Kawakawa in 1975 and lived in the bush nearby for the rest of his life. In 1999 he designed the town's new public toilets as his gift back — mosaic tiles, bottle walls, curved organic lines, a grass roof, and his signature refusal of straight lines. They are still in use, still free, and genuinely remarkable. The Hundertwasser Memorial Park is a short walk away, and the town now has a Hundertwasser-themed cultural centre. If you stop, stop for thirty minutes, not two.

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at Kawakawa Hundertwasser Toilets is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. The most photographed public toilets in New Zealand — designed by Austrian artist Friedensreich Hundertwasser, 1999. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

pois.name: Ninety Mile Beach / Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē
pois.short: It's 88 km long, not 90 miles, and it is a legal public highway — though rental cars are almost always banned.
pois.commentary: Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē — 'the long beach of Tōhē' — runs 88 km up the west side of the Aupōuri Peninsula. The 'ninety mile' name comes from early missionaries who misjudged the distance their horses could travel in a day. It is gazetted as a public road, and tour buses drive it at low tide, but tides, quicksand and unmarked creek outlets have destroyed a lot of cars; nearly every rental agreement prohibits driving on it. See it from Te Paki Stream (boogie-board the giant sand dunes) or from the Scott Point lookout at the northern end.
sources_to_check: DOC — Te Paki / Ninety Mile Beach
Te Hiku o Te Ika — Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē board

reviews.summary: Ninety Mile Beach / Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. It's 88 km long, not 90 miles, and it is a legal public highway — though rental cars are almost always banned. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Kai Iwi Lakes / Taharoa Domain
pois.short: Three ancient dune lakes with white-sand bottoms and water so clear it looks tropical.
pois.commentary: The Kai Iwi Lakes — Taharoa, Waikare and Kai Iwi — are windblown dune lakes, fed only by rainwater sitting on top of an impermeable iron-pan layer deep in the sand. The result is freshwater so clear it looks like it belongs in the Mediterranean, over a pure white quartz-sand lake bottom. They're estimated at 1.8 million years old. The Taharoa Domain has a campground, picnic areas, and sheltered swimming; Lake Taharoa is the biggest and warmest. The lakes are taonga to Te Roroa. Keep motorised craft out of the smaller lakes.

reviews.summary: Kai Iwi Lakes / Taharoa Domain draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Three ancient dune lakes with white-sand bottoms and water so clear it looks tropical. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Rawene Car Ferry
pois.short: The 15-minute Hokianga Harbour ferry — NZ's oldest continuous car-ferry service, running since the 1920s.
pois.commentary: Rawene is the third-oldest European settlement in New Zealand (1830s) and perches on a spit into the Hokianga. The car ferry *Kohu* crosses the harbour between Rawene and Kohukohu every hour or so — a ~15 minute ride that saves drivers on SH12 a two-hour detour around the harbour. While you wait, walk the waterfront: Clendon House (1860s) is open to visitors, and the Boatshed Café floats on pontoons. The ferry has been running in one form or another since the 1920s.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Rawene Car Ferry — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The 15-minute Hokianga Harbour ferry — NZ's oldest continuous car-ferry service, running since the 1920s. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Matauri Bay / Rainbow Warrior Memorial
pois.short: The headland memorial to the Greenpeace ship *Rainbow Warrior*, bombed by French agents in 1985 and resting in the sea below.
pois.commentary: On 10 July 1985 the Greenpeace flagship *Rainbow Warrior* was bombed in Auckland Harbour by agents of the French DGSE. Photographer Fernando Pereira was killed. After she was raised, iwi Ngāti Kura offered a final resting place: the ship was scuttled off the Cavalli Islands in December 1987 and is now one of New Zealand's best dive sites. A hilltop memorial above Matauri Bay — steel propellers and a carved stone — looks out over the exact water she lies in. The bay itself is one of the most beautiful on the east coast: white sand, clear water, almost no development.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Matauri Bay / Rainbow Warrior Memorial — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The headland memorial to the Greenpeace ship *Rainbow Warrior*, bombed by French agents in 1985 and resting in the sea below. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Bay of Islands / Pēwhairangi
pois.short: 144 islands, early contact between Māori and Europeans, and some of the North Island's best sailing water.
pois.commentary: The Bay of Islands has 144 islands within a 16 km radius of Paihia. In 1769 Cook's *Endeavour* anchored here and he gave the bay its English name. From around 1800 it became the main point of contact between Māori and European whalers, traders and missionaries — which is why Waitangi, Kerikeri, Russell and Paihia are all clustered on this one bay. Today it's a sailing, fishing, and tour-boat centre: Cape Brett / Hole in the Rock, Urupukapuka Island, swimming with dolphins (heavily regulated), and the passenger ferry between Paihia and Russell.

reviews.summary: Bay of Islands / Pēwhairangi draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. 144 islands, early contact between Māori and Europeans, and some of the North Island's best sailing water. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Poor Knights Islands / Tawhiti Rahi
pois.short: Jacques Cousteau called these 'one of the top ten dive sites in the world'.
pois.commentary: Marine reserve since 1981; once home to a Ngātiwai population, abandoned after 1820s raids and now permanently tapu. No landing permitted.
pois.audio_story: You are looking out toward the Poor Knights Islands, Tawhiti Rahi and Aorangi, two volcanic remnants rising sheer from the sea about twenty three kilometres off the Tutukaka coast. They are uninhabited and have been for almost two hundred years. In the 1820s, a series of raids ended the Ngātiwai community that lived here. Out of respect for the people who died, the islands were declared tapu, and to this day no one is permitted to land. What you can do is dive. The waters around the Poor Knights are warmed by the East Auckland Current, which carries subtropical species far further south than they would otherwise reach. Underwater you will find the largest sea cave in the world, kelp forests, schools of pink mau mau, and the strange spectacle of a mid latitude reef alive with tropical fish. The marine reserve has been fully protected since 1981, and the difference shows in every dive. Jacques Cousteau, who saw a great deal of ocean in his life, once placed these islands among the top ten dive sites on Earth. From shore the cliffs look almost severe, but beneath the surface, life is everywhere.
sources_to_check: DOC — Poor Knights Islands Marine Reserve

reviews.summary: Rated one of the world's top ten dive sites by Jacques Cousteau — Poor Knights delivers extraordinary marine biodiversity in towering sea caves and arches. Reviewers say visibility can exceed 30 metres. Even snorkellers and glass-bottom boat visitors come away blown away by the underwater life.

pois.name: Tutukaka Coast
pois.short: Northland's diving capital — the launch point for the Poor Knights, with a string of golden bays.
pois.commentary: The Tutukaka coast strings together a series of east coast bays — Matapouri, Whale Bay, Sandy Bay — each more photogenic than the last. The marina is the launch point for almost every dive trip out to the Poor Knights.
pois.audio_story: You are driving the Tutukaka coast, a curving thirty kilometre strip of east Northland that the locals quietly believe is the best stretch of coastline in the country. They might be right. Each headland reveals another bay. Matapouri sits around a perfect crescent of white sand, with safe summer swimming for kids. Just behind it a short bush walk leads to Whale Bay, smaller, often empty, and good for finding your own patch of sand. Further north Sandy Bay is the local surfing favourite. The hub is Tutukaka itself, a marina village wedged between green hills and a deep natural harbour. From this marina almost every dive trip and fishing charter in the area heads out, most of them bound for the Poor Knights Islands twenty three kilometres offshore. If you have a day, take a boat trip. If you have an afternoon, just bay hop. Stop at Schnappa Rock for fish and chips with a glass of something cold. There is no urgent itinerary out here, and that is rather the point.

reviews.summary: Tutukaka Coast draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Northland's diving capital — the launch point for the Poor Knights, with a string of golden bays. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Whangārei Falls / Otuihau
pois.short: A 26m basalt waterfall right inside Whangārei city — five minute walk from the carpark.
pois.commentary: Otuihau is a 26m vertical waterfall over a basalt cliff. It is one of the photographed waterfalls in NZ because it is both impressive and incredibly accessible.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Whangārei Falls, known to local Māori as Otuihau. The falls drop twenty six metres in a single clean curtain over a basalt cliff and into a wide pool surrounded by ferns. They sit inside the suburb of Tikipunga, only ten minutes drive from central Whangārei, and yet they look like something you should have hiked half a day to find. From the carpark a paved path takes you to the top of the falls in under a minute. Continue down the steps and you will reach two viewing platforms at the base, where you can feel the spray on your face. If you have an hour to spare, the Hātea River walk loops away from the falls through native bush, past old Māori pā sites and along the river that drains the Whangārei plain. The water you are watching tonight will be in the harbour by morning. It is one of the easiest pieces of beauty in the country to reach. Take the short walk and the long photograph.

reviews.summary: Whangārei Falls / Otuihau draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A 26m basalt waterfall right inside Whangārei city — five minute walk from the carpark. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Clendon House, Rawene
pois.short: An 1860s house above the Hokianga, kept by Heritage NZ exactly as the Clendon family left it.
pois.commentary: Built in the 1860s for James Reddy Clendon, U.S. Consul and one of the witnesses to the Treaty of Waitangi. His wife Jane Takotowi Cochrane, of Ngāpuhi descent, raised eight children here after his death in 1872 and lived in the house until 1919.
pois.audio_story: You are standing in front of Clendon House, a wooden colonial home with a wide veranda and a view straight down the Hokianga Harbour. The house was built in the 1860s for James Reddy Clendon, a remarkable figure who was at one time the United States Consul in the Bay of Islands and who signed the Treaty of Waitangi as a witness on the morning of the sixth of February eighteen forty. He died at this house in 1872, leaving his much younger wife, Jane Takotowi Cochrane of Ngāpuhi descent, with eight children and the family debts. Jane stayed. She raised the children, ran the property, and lived in this house until 1919. Almost everything you will see inside, the books, the china, the dresses, the letters, was hers. Heritage New Zealand has cared for the house since the 1970s and has changed almost nothing. You are walking through a Victorian Māori-Pākehā family home that survived because one widow refused to let it go. Sit on the veranda before you leave. The harbour view has not changed in a century and a half.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Clendon House, Rawene — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. An 1860s house above the Hokianga, kept by Heritage NZ exactly as the Clendon family left it. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Koutu Boulders, Hokianga
pois.short: Spherical boulders bigger than the Moeraki ones, but almost no one knows they exist.
pois.commentary: Hokianga's answer to Moeraki — large concretions exposed at low tide on the inner harbour shore at Koutu, several over 3m across. Access is across private land with permission expected.
pois.audio_story: You are looking for the Koutu Boulders, and unlike their famous cousins on the Otago coast at Moeraki, almost no one has heard of these. The Koutu Boulders sit on the Hokianga Harbour shoreline, rounded grey concretions that grew slowly inside soft mudstone over millions of years. As the cliffs eroded, the boulders were released onto the beach. Some are over three metres across, larger than any of the Moeraki examples. They are best seen at low tide, and you will need to time your visit with the tide chart. Access is along a quiet road north of Opononi, then across a stretch of foreshore that crosses private land, so be respectful, take nothing, and follow any signs. While you are out here, take in the wider Hokianga, one of the most spiritually significant harbours in the country. According to Ngāpuhi tradition, this is where Kupe the navigator first arrived, and from where he eventually left these islands to return to Hawaiki. Stand on the sand, look at the boulders, look at the harbour, and consider the weight of all that history settling around you.

reviews.summary: Koutu Boulders, Hokianga draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Spherical boulders bigger than the Moeraki ones, but almost no one knows they exist. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Trounson Kauri Park
pois.short: A 40 minute loop through one of the best surviving stands of mature kauri — and the best place near Waipoua to hear kiwi at night.
pois.commentary: 586-hectare DOC reserve. Mainland Island predator control has allowed kiwi populations to recover; guided night walks have become a draw.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Trounson Kauri Park, just south of the Waipoua Forest. If Waipoua holds the giants like Tāne Mahuta, Trounson holds the family — a gentler, more intimate forest of mature kauri, podocarp, and tree fern, looped by a flat forty minute boardwalk. The park is named for James Trounson, a sawmiller who, in a moment of unusual foresight, gifted this remnant to the Crown in 1919 instead of cutting it down. Department of Conservation rangers have run intensive predator control here for decades, and the result has been a dramatic recovery of native birds and, especially, of brown kiwi. After dark the forest changes character entirely. Kiwi call to each other across the gloom, more sheep dog than the songbird people imagine. Local guided night walks operate from the campground, and they are the most reliable way in the North Island to actually see a kiwi in the wild. Before you walk, scrub your boots at the cleaning station. Kauri dieback disease has no cure. The trees you are about to meet are over a thousand years old, and they need every visitor to do this one small thing.

reviews.summary: Trounson Kauri Park draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A 40 minute loop through one of the best surviving stands of mature kauri — and the best place near Waipoua to hear kiwi at night. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Mangōnui Fish Shop
pois.short: Fish and chips on a wharf jutting into Doubtless Bay — voted New Zealand's best more times than anyone has bothered to count.
pois.commentary: The Mangōnui Fish Shop is a green weatherboard building extending out over the harbour. Open since the 1940s; consistently rated among NZ's top fish and chip shops.
pois.audio_story: You are at the Mangōnui Fish Shop, a small green weatherboard building that has been pushing out into the harbour on wooden piles for the better part of a century. The fish on your plate was, in many cases, swimming in this same bay yesterday. Mangōnui sits on Doubtless Bay, named by Captain Cook in seventeen sixty nine. Cook never actually entered the bay. From offshore he wrote in his journal that it was doubtless a bay, and the name stuck, slightly absurd and entirely permanent. The fish shop has been here since the 1940s, and the formula has not really changed. Order the snapper and chips. Take it outside to one of the wooden tables suspended over the water. Watch the gulls calculate their odds. If you are lucky a stingray will glide past below the deck. The chips are golden, the fish is fresh, the harbour is quiet, and the only argument any traveller has after eating here is whether this or some rival down south is the country's best. Sit a little longer than you planned. There is no hurry in Mangōnui.

reviews.summary: Mangōnui Fish Shop delivers an excellent food and wine experience that visitors describe as a highlight of the region. Fish and chips on a wharf jutting into Doubtless Bay — voted New Zealand's best more times than anyone has bothered to count. Reviewers praise the quality of produce and the setting, and recommend combining it with nearby cellar doors or restaurants.

pois.name: Stone Store & Kemp House, Kerikeri
pois.short: The two oldest buildings in New Zealand — a wooden mission house from 1822 and a stone store from 1832.
pois.commentary: Kemp House (1822) is the oldest surviving timber building in NZ. The neighbouring Stone Store (1832) is the oldest stone building. Both Heritage New Zealand sites in the Kerikeri Mission Station precinct.
pois.audio_story: You are standing at the Kerikeri Basin, where two unassuming buildings carry the title of the oldest surviving structures of European New Zealand. The white wooden house is Kemp House. It was built in eighteen twenty two by missionary carpenters as a home for the Reverend John Butler. The Kemp family lived here for the next hundred and fifty years. Almost everything inside, the rocking chair, the kitchen, the children's beds, came from that long occupation. Next door, the squat grey building is the Stone Store, completed in eighteen thirty two. It is the oldest stone building in the country. It was originally built to hold mission supplies and trade goods, and it still operates as a working store on the ground floor today. You can buy a pound of rope and a tin of biscuits and pretend, briefly, that it is eighteen sixty. Across the river, on the slope above the basin, sits Kororipo Pā, the home of the great Ngāpuhi rangatira Hongi Hika, who invited the missionaries here in eighteen nineteen. Walk up. Look back. Two civilisations met on this small piece of ground, and what followed shaped everything.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Stone Store & Kemp House, Kerikeri — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The two oldest buildings in New Zealand — a wooden mission house from 1822 and a stone store from 1832. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Te Paki Sand Dunes
pois.short: A miniature Sahara at the top of New Zealand — sand dunes you can hire a board for and ride down.
pois.commentary: Giant dunes at the northern end of Ninety Mile Beach near Te Paki Stream. Common stop on Cape Reinga bus tours; sandboarding is the standard activity.
pois.audio_story: You have come to the top of New Zealand and arrived in a desert. The Te Paki sand dunes rise behind Ninety Mile Beach in great pale ridges, some over a hundred metres high, scoured into endless ripples by the wind off the Tasman. Stand at the bottom and look up, and you would not believe you are still in the same country as the rainforests of Northland. Hire a sandboard from one of the operators along the access road, climb until your calves burn, and ride down the slipface on your stomach. Most travellers come out of the dunes laughing and shaking sand out of places sand should not be. The Te Paki Stream below is, technically, a public road. Tour buses drive its sandy bed to reach the southern end of Ninety Mile Beach. The dunes are still moving. They are slowly burying the small ngahere, the bush remnants, behind them. There is something humbling about a landscape that simply will not stay still. Brush the sand off, drink some water, and remember that Cape Reinga is only twenty more minutes north.

reviews.summary: Te Paki Sand Dunes draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. A miniature Sahara at the top of New Zealand — sand dunes you can hire a board for and ride down. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Ngawha Springs
pois.short: Sixteen iwi-owned mineral pools in a swamp behind Kaikohe — the most untouristed hot pools in New Zealand.
pois.commentary: Owned by the local hapū of Ngāpuhi (Te Uri o Hua), the Ngawha Springs complex is one of the few NZ geothermal fields outside the Taupō Volcanic Zone. Rustic, mineral-heavy waters in wooden pools.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Ngawha, and you should set your expectations for something honest rather than something polished. There are sixteen pools here, each a different temperature, a different mineral profile, a different colour. Some are clear, some are inky black, some are sulphur green. The pools are owned by the local hapū of Ngāpuhi, who have used these waters for healing for centuries, and they have chosen to keep the place rustic. The boardwalks are wooden, the changing sheds are basic, and the experience is the experience. The geology here is unusual. Almost every other natural hot spring in the North Island sits in the Taupō Volcanic Zone, fed by the line of volcanoes from White Island to Ruapehu. Ngawha is hundreds of kilometres outside that zone, fed by an entirely different system of deep faults. The locals will tell you that each pool is good for a different ailment. The water smells of sulphur. Your skin will be soft for two days. Soak slowly, move from pool to pool, and try to think about nothing at all.

reviews.summary: Ngawha Springs draws visitors back time and again for its natural beauty and peaceful atmosphere. Sixteen iwi-owned mineral pools in a swamp behind Kaikohe — the most untouristed hot pools in New Zealand. Reviewers praise the walking tracks and the sense of being away from it all, and recommend visiting on weekdays to get the experience at its best.

pois.name: Ruapekapeka Pā
pois.short: The last battle of the Northern War — a remarkably preserved 1846 Ngāpuhi fortress that outsmarted the British Empire's artillery.
pois.commentary: Ruapekapeka was built by Kawiti in 1845 as an engineering marvel: deep trench and tunnel system designed to survive artillery bombardment. British forces took the empty pā on 11 January 1846 after Ngāpuhi retreated. One of the best-preserved nineteenth century pā sites in the country.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Ruapekapeka, the hidden bat's nest, and you are standing at the site of the final battle of the Northern War of eighteen forty five to eighteen forty six. The chief Kawiti, an older ally of Hone Heke, built this pā here in the back country south of the Bay of Islands in eighteen forty five. What he built was an extraordinary piece of military engineering. Deep defensive trenches, a network of underground tunnels where warriors could shelter during artillery bombardment, and palisade walls sloped inward to deflect cannon balls. The British brought the largest artillery force ever assembled in New Zealand to reduce it. For ten days in December eighteen forty five and January eighteen forty six they shelled the pā without breaking its defences. On the eleventh of January, the British troops crept into a pā Kawiti had quietly evacuated the previous evening. They had won the ground and lost the war. Today the site is extraordinarily well preserved. You can walk the trench lines, stand in the reconstructed palisade, and read the story on excellent interpretive panels. A working waharoa, carved entrance, welcomes visitors. Treat the site as the memorial it is.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Ruapekapeka Pā as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The last battle of the Northern War — a remarkably preserved 1846 Ngāpuhi fortress that outsmarted the British Empire's artillery. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Pompallier Mission, Russell
pois.short: New Zealand's oldest surviving industrial building — the 1842 rammed-earth printery of French bishop Pompallier's Catholic mission.
pois.commentary: Built 1842 from rammed earth using French pisé-de-terre technique, Pompallier House housed a working printery that produced around 30,000 books in te reo Māori before closing in 1850.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Pompallier Mission in Russell, and you are looking at the oldest industrial building still standing in New Zealand. The building was constructed in eighteen forty two by the French Catholic bishop Jean Baptiste Pompallier, who arrived in the Bay of Islands in eighteen thirty eight with the intention of converting Māori to Catholicism in direct competition with the already established Anglican Church Missionary Society. The building itself is unique in New Zealand. It was built using pisé de terre, a French technique of rammed earth construction, because Pompallier's brothers were French craftsmen who knew how to build with the materials they knew. Inside, a working printery and tannery produced around thirty thousand books, mostly religious texts translated into te reo Māori, that were then bound in leather from local cattle. The mission closed in eighteen fifty when Pompallier moved his headquarters to Auckland. Heritage New Zealand has restored the building and the printery still operates demonstration printings on the original equipment. Allow an hour. Watch a printing demonstration if one is running. The paper, ink, leather, and type were all made or set right here.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Pompallier Mission, Russell as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. New Zealand's oldest surviving industrial building — the 1842 rammed-earth printery of French bishop Pompallier's Catholic mission. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Te Waimate Mission
pois.short: The second-oldest surviving European building in New Zealand — 1832 timber mission house, and the site of the country's first Anglican bishopric.
pois.commentary: Built 1832 by the Church Missionary Society as part of an agricultural mission station. New Zealand's first Anglican Bishop George Selwyn lived here from 1842-1844. The first ploughed paddock in New Zealand was prepared here.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Te Waimate Mission, sixteen kilometres inland from Kerikeri, and you are standing on the site of the first European style farm in New Zealand. The Church Missionary Society established Te Waimate in eighteen thirty as an agricultural mission, a deliberate attempt to teach local Māori European farming techniques alongside Christianity. By eighteen thirty two there were three timber houses on the property and the first ploughed paddock in the country had been prepared here. One of those houses still stands and is now one of the two oldest surviving European buildings in New Zealand, along with the nearby Kemp House in Kerikeri. Te Waimate became the site of New Zealand's first Anglican bishopric when George Selwyn arrived in eighteen forty two to establish a training college for Māori clergy. Selwyn lived here for two years before moving the seat to Auckland. The station fell into decline in the late eighteen forties. Today Heritage New Zealand has restored the mission house, the little wooden church of Saint John the Baptist next door, and the surrounding gardens. A visit here, away from the Bay of Islands crowds, rewards the careful visitor with a quiet sense of the earliest years of the mission period.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Te Waimate Mission — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The second-oldest surviving European building in New Zealand — 1832 timber mission house, and the site of the country's first Anglican bishopric. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Mangungu Mission
pois.short: The Wesleyan mission house where more chiefs signed Te Tiriti o Waitangi than anywhere else — 70 signatures on 12 February 1840.
pois.commentary: Wesleyan mission at Hōreke on the southern Hokianga, established 1828. On 12 February 1840 Mangungu hosted the largest single Treaty of Waitangi signing event, with approximately 70 Hokianga chiefs signing Te Tiriti.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Mangungu Mission House on the southern shore of the Hokianga Harbour. Mangungu is remarkable for one particular reason. On the twelfth of February eighteen forty, just six days after the Treaty of Waitangi was first signed at Waitangi Bay on the other coast, approximately seventy chiefs from the Hokianga iwi gathered here and added their signatures to Te Tiriti. It was the largest single signing event in the Treaty process. For decades the role of Mangungu in the Treaty's signing was under recognised, overshadowed by the more famous signing at Waitangi. That has changed. The mission house itself, built around eighteen thirty nine by Wesleyan Methodists, has been restored by Heritage New Zealand and is a beautiful example of colonial timber construction. The garden slopes down to the water. A small cemetery holds missionaries and early settlers. Every year on the twelfth of February, a commemoration is held here. It is quieter than the events at Waitangi Bay, but the people who come to this one understand exactly what happened at this place, and why it matters. Allow an hour. The drive down to Hōreke is itself lovely.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Mangungu Mission as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The Wesleyan mission house where more chiefs signed Te Tiriti o Waitangi than anywhere else — 70 signatures on 12 February 1840. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Flagstaff Hill, Russell / Maiki
pois.short: The hill above Russell where Ngāpuhi chief Hone Heke famously cut down the British flagpole four times — sparking the Northern War.
pois.commentary: Maiki Hill overlooks Russell. Ngāpuhi chief Hone Heke cut down the British flagpole here in July 1844 and three more times over subsequent months, the fourth cutting on 11 March 1845 triggering open war.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the top of Flagstaff Hill, known to Māori as Maiki, looking directly down over the little town of Russell and out across the Bay of Islands. This unassuming low hill was the site of one of the most symbolic acts of resistance in early colonial New Zealand. In July eighteen forty four, the Ngāpuhi chief Hone Heke rode up here with an axe and cut down the British flagpole. He did it again. And again. And again. The fourth and final cutting on the eleventh of March eighteen forty five came as part of a coordinated attack on Russell itself that touched off what became known as the Northern War. For Heke, the flagpole was a symbol of the economic marginalisation of Ngāpuhi after the capital had moved south to Auckland, draining customs revenue and trade away from the Bay of Islands. Cutting it down was a calculated political act, not a vandalism. Today a flagpole stands here again, erected by Ngāpuhi themselves as part of the healing of the long relationship. Stand here, look down on the harbour, and imagine the sound of axe on wood in eighteen forty four.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Flagstaff Hill, Russell / Maiki — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The hill above Russell where Ngāpuhi chief Hone Heke famously cut down the British flagpole four times — sparking the Northern War. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Motuarohia / Roberton Island — Cook's First Landing
pois.short: The small island where James Cook first came ashore in the Bay of Islands in 1769 — the site now better known for its twin lagoons.
pois.commentary: On 29 November 1769 Cook anchored off Motuarohia and came ashore with Banks and Solander. The island was the scene of a complex multi-canoe encounter with local Ngāpuhi. Now part of the Bay of Islands Maritime Park.
pois.audio_story: You are looking at Motuarohia, sometimes called Roberton Island, sitting in the middle of the Bay of Islands. On the twenty ninth of November seventeen sixty nine, James Cook anchored the Endeavour in the lee of this island and came ashore with his naturalists Joseph Banks and Daniel Solander. It was the first European landing anywhere in what is now the Northland region, and one of the earliest meetings between Cook and Māori. The encounter was tense and nearly ended badly. A fleet of around three hundred warriors in several large waka surrounded the Endeavour's longboat in the shallows. Banks later wrote that Cook fired warning shots over their heads and the situation was defused by the bravery and skill of the Tahitian translator Tupaia, who was able to speak with the local Ngāpuhi in a mutually understandable dialect. Today Motuarohia is best known to visitors for its twin aquamarine lagoons on the northern side, reachable by boat tours from Paihia. A short walking track climbs to a lookout between the two bays with a spectacular view. Swim, snorkel, walk up to the lookout, and remember that this is where Cook first put a boot down in the north of the country.
sources_to_check: DOC — Bay of Islands Maritime Park

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Motuarohia / Roberton Island — Cook's First Landing — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The small island where James Cook first came ashore in the Bay of Islands in 1769 — the site now better known for its twin lagoons. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Christ Church, Russell
pois.short: New Zealand's oldest surviving church — built 1836, with musket-ball holes in the timber walls from the 1845 sacking of Russell.
pois.commentary: Built 1836 as an Anglican church and community building at Kororāreka. Charles Darwin donated to its construction during the Beagle's visit in December 1835. Holes in the walls are from the 1845 sacking.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Christ Church in Russell, New Zealand's oldest surviving church. The timber walls you are looking at were raised in eighteen thirty six, when Kororāreka, as the town was then known, was the rough and notorious trading port of the South Pacific, full of whalers, deserters, and grog shops. The church was built as both a religious building and a community hall for the small settler population. Among the early donors to its construction was the young naturalist Charles Darwin, who visited Kororāreka in December eighteen thirty five aboard HMS Beagle and disapproved heartily of the settlement's debauchery. He gave, in his own words, a small sum toward the chapel. Look closely at the weatherboards on the outside of the building. Several of them show holes made by musket balls during the sacking of Kororāreka on the eleventh of March eighteen forty five. Hone Heke and Kawiti's men had orders not to damage the church, and for the most part they did not, but some stray fire hit the walls. The church is still a working parish. Walk quietly around the small cemetery. A surprising number of sailors and whalers rest here, including some killed in eighteen forty five.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Christ Church, Russell — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. New Zealand's oldest surviving church — built 1836, with musket-ball holes in the timber walls from the 1845 sacking of Russell. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Mangōnui Historic Precinct
pois.short: A whaling and trading port of the 1830s preserved almost entirely — Doubtless Bay's timber-fronted main street tells a hundred and ninety years of story.
pois.commentary: Mangōnui on Doubtless Bay was a major Pacific whaling port from the 1830s. The timber storefronts of the main street are the most intact surviving whaling-era commercial precinct in the country.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived in Mangōnui on the southern shore of Doubtless Bay. In the eighteen thirties and forties this was one of the major whaling ports of the southern Pacific. Hundreds of American, British, French, and Australian whaling ships called here each year to reprovision, to trade with local Ngāti Kahu, and to repair before heading back out onto the Pacific whaling grounds. The main street of Mangōnui preserves more of that period, in continuous occupation, than almost anywhere else in the country. The timber storefronts with their deep verandas and false parapets, the old waterfront trading store, the stone wharf, all survive largely intact. The town's famous fish and chip shop, which claims to be the world's best, operates out of a building that was originally a customs house. Walk the length of the main street slowly. Read the Heritage New Zealand plaques on the buildings. Eat fish and chips on the wharf, watching the small fleet at rest. Mangōnui rewards an unhurried afternoon more than most New Zealand small towns. Then drive five minutes west to Butler Point for the whaling museum, which is the essential companion to the main street.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Mangōnui Historic Precinct — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A whaling and trading port of the 1830s preserved almost entirely — Doubtless Bay's timber-fronted main street tells a hundred and ninety years of story. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Butler Point Whaling Museum
pois.short: The 1843 home of Captain William Butler — a Pacific whaling captain who retired at Mangōnui — preserved with its original furniture and whaling artefacts.
pois.commentary: William Butler was an English whaling captain who settled at Mangōnui in 1836 and built the Butler Point house in 1843. The house and its whaling museum preserve one of the best collections of Pacific whaling artefacts in the country.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Butler Point, on the southern side of Mangōnui Harbour. Butler Point is where Captain William Butler, an English whaling captain, built a home and trading station in the eighteen thirties and forties after deciding Doubtless Bay was where he wanted to spend the second half of his life. Butler arrived in the Pacific whaling grounds in the eighteen twenties. By the mid eighteen thirties he had developed good trading relationships with Ngāti Kahu and was serving as a pilot, interpreter, and provisioner to the scores of whaling ships that called at Mangōnui. In eighteen forty three he built the house that now anchors the museum. The property has been kept in the Butler family for more than a century and a half, and is now open to visitors by appointment. The collection of whaling artefacts, the harpoons, the tryworks for boiling oil, the scrimshaw carved on sperm whale teeth, the personal letters of the captain, is one of the best collections of Pacific whaling material in the country. The setting is idyllic. A guided tour by a descendant of the captain is the standard experience. Call ahead to book.

reviews.summary: The cultural experience at Butler Point Whaling Museum is praised for being both accessible and genuinely informative. The 1843 home of Captain William Butler — a Pacific whaling captain who retired at Mangōnui — preserved with its original furniture and whaling artefacts. Visitors appreciate the quality of the interpretation and often say they learned something they didn't expect to.

pois.name: First Anglican Church Site, Paihia
pois.short: The site of Henry Williams' 1823 mission — where Samuel Marsden first preached in Aotearoa was nearby, and te reo Māori was first written down.
pois.commentary: The third CMS mission station was established at Paihia by Henry Williams in 1823. The bilingual Williams family translated the Bible and the Treaty of Waitangi into te reo Māori here.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the site of the first Paihia mission, established in eighteen twenty three by the Church Missionary Society under the redoubtable Henry Williams. The mission is no longer here in its original form, but interpretive boards on the waterfront set out the story. What happened at Paihia shaped the course of early colonial New Zealand in ways that are still being felt. Henry Williams and his brother William were fluent speakers of te reo Māori, and along with their wives they produced the first written Māori language grammars, the first te reo translation of the Bible, and, crucially, the Māori language text of the Treaty of Waitangi, which was largely drafted in a small timber house that once stood roughly where the current Anglican Church of St Paul now stands. The translation of the Treaty at Paihia, the choices Williams made in how to render English concepts such as sovereignty into a te reo framework that Māori could understand, have been debated and litigated for almost two centuries. Stand on the waterfront, look across at Waitangi just a few kilometres away, and let that weight sit on the landscape for a moment.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe First Anglican Church Site, Paihia as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The site of Henry Williams' 1823 mission — where Samuel Marsden first preached in Aotearoa was nearby, and te reo Māori was first written down. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Stone Store, Kerikeri (history focus)
pois.short: The 1832 Stone Store and 1822 Kemp House — the two oldest surviving European buildings in New Zealand, standing side by side on the Kerikeri River.
pois.commentary: Kemp House was built 1821-22 as a missionary home. The Stone Store (1832-36) was built as a mission warehouse from Sydney-cut basalt. Both remain in their original positions on the Kerikeri Basin.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the Kerikeri Basin, and you are standing in front of the two oldest surviving European buildings in New Zealand. Kemp House, the timber mission house, was built between eighteen twenty one and eighteen twenty two. Beside it stands the Stone Store, built between eighteen thirty two and eighteen thirty six from basalt cut in Sydney and shipped across the Tasman. Together they tell the story of the earliest Church Missionary Society presence in the country. The mission here was established in eighteen nineteen under the protection of the paramount chief Hongi Hika, who saw advantage in hosting Europeans who could trade and teach literacy. The store was intended to be a warehouse for goods traded with Māori and incoming ships, but by the time it was completed the original commercial model had already become obsolete and the building spent most of its long life as a general store and, later, a private home. Heritage New Zealand now operates both buildings as a museum. Inside the Stone Store the original creaking floorboards and the smell of old stone are undiminished. Kemp House is small and perfectly preserved. Allow at least an hour.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Stone Store, Kerikeri (history focus) — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. The 1832 Stone Store and 1822 Kemp House — the two oldest surviving European buildings in New Zealand, standing side by side on the Kerikeri River. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Clendon House, Rāwene
pois.short: The home of James Clendon, US Consul and Treaty of Waitangi signatory — a 1860s Hokianga house preserved almost entirely unchanged.
pois.commentary: Built 1866-68 for James Reddy Clendon, British ship owner who served as first US Consul to NZ and signed the Treaty of Waitangi. The house passed to his Māori wife Jane Takotowi Cochrane. Run by Heritage NZ.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Clendon House in Rāwene on the southern shore of the Hokianga Harbour. The house was built between eighteen sixty six and eighteen sixty eight for James Reddy Clendon, one of the more remarkable figures of the early colonial period. Clendon was an English ship owner who had moved to Kororāreka in the eighteen thirties, befriended local Māori and chiefs, served as the first United States Consul in New Zealand, witnessed the signing of the Treaty of Waitangi in eighteen forty, and after losing his fortune moved his family to the quieter Hokianga. The house was built for his second wife, Jane Takotowi Cochrane, a rangatira of the local Te Māhurehure hapū, a generation younger than him. When James died in eighteen seventy two Jane inherited the house and raised their eight children here. She lived in the house for another fifty years. Because the property remained in the family until gifted to Heritage New Zealand in the nineteen seventies, an extraordinary quantity of the original furniture, papers, and personal items survive. Walking through Clendon House is the closest thing to stepping into an intact colonial home you will find in the country.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Clendon House, Rāwene as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The home of James Clendon, US Consul and Treaty of Waitangi signatory — a 1860s Hokianga house preserved almost entirely unchanged. pois.name: Kororāreka Memorial to the Fallen
pois.short: A simple stone memorial in Russell to the defenders and attackers of the 1845 sacking of Kororāreka — remembering both sides.
pois.commentary: On 11 March 1845 Ngāpuhi forces under Kawiti, Hone Heke, and Pumuka attacked Kororāreka. The town was largely destroyed. Casualties on both sides. The memorial in the modern town acknowledges both Pākehā and Māori dead.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the Kororāreka Memorial in the centre of modern Russell. The stone acknowledges the dead of both sides on the eleventh of March eighteen forty five, the day known to history as the sack of Kororāreka. On that morning, Ngāpuhi forces under three chiefs, Hone Heke, Kawiti, and Pumuka, attacked the settlement in a carefully coordinated three pronged assault. Most of the town was burned. The small British garrison was driven from its blockhouse. HMS Hazard in the harbour was disabled. The civilian population fled aboard American and British ships and were rescued to Auckland. Pumuka, the third chief in the attack, was killed in the fighting, as were several British marines and settlers. For Ngāpuhi, the sack was a calculated demonstration of military power in response to colonial economic policies that had devastated the Bay of Islands economy. For the settlers, it was a catastrophe. The Northern War that followed cost several hundred lives over the next ten months. The memorial was erected in the twentieth century to name the dead on both sides, not only the British casualties. A small but important piece of modern memorial work.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Kororāreka Memorial to the Fallen — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A simple stone memorial in Russell to the defenders and attackers of the 1845 sacking of Kororāreka — remembering both sides. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Kororāreka / Russell
pois.short: The first European capital of NZ — sacked by Hōne Heke in 1845 in the opening act of the Northern War.
pois.commentary: Kororāreka was a raucous whaling port before Hōne Heke Pōkai chopped down the British flagstaff on Maiki Hill four times between 1844 and 1845, triggering the Northern War. The attack on 11 March 1845 destroyed much of the town. Today Russell is a quiet harbourside village, but the hillside pā terraces, Christ Church (NZ's oldest surviving church, still pocked with musket-ball holes), and the flagstaff replica on the hill all tell the story of the first armed resistance to Crown sovereignty.

pois.name: Te Maiki / Flagstaff Hill
pois.short: The hill where Hōne Heke cut down the British flagstaff four times — the spark that lit the Northern War.
pois.commentary: Te Maiki was originally a Ngāpuhi pā site commanding views over Kororāreka and the Bay of Islands. When the British erected a flagstaff to fly the Union Jack, Hōne Heke saw it as a symbol of sovereignty over Māori. He felled it four times between July 1844 and March 1845. The fourth felling was a diversionary tactic for the sacking of Kororāreka below. A replica flagstaff now flies the NZ flag. The walk up takes about 15 minutes and offers sweeping harbour views.

pois.name: Tāne Mahuta
pois.short: Lord of the Forest — the largest living kauri tree, named for the god who separated earth and sky.
pois.commentary: Tāne Mahuta stands 51 metres tall with a girth of 13.8 metres in the Waipoua Forest, estimated at 1,250–2,500 years old. Named for the atua (god) Tāne, who in Māori creation tradition lay between his parents Ranginui (sky father) and Papatūānuku (earth mother) and pushed them apart to let light into the world. For Te Roroa, the local iwi, the tree is a living ancestor. A short boardwalk from SH12 leads to the viewing platform.

pois.name: Mangungu Mission House
pois.short: Where the largest single signing of Te Tiriti o Waitangi took place — 70 Hokianga chiefs signed here on 12 February 1840.
pois.commentary: On 12 February 1840, just six days after the first signing at Waitangi, about 70 chiefs gathered on the grounds of the Wesleyan mission house at Mangungu to debate and sign Te Tiriti. It was the largest single signing event. The 1838 kauri mission house still stands on its hill above the Hokianga Harbour, managed by Heritage New Zealand.

pois.name: Arai-te-Uru Recreation Reserve
pois.short: Where the great navigator Kupe first set foot on Aotearoa — the traditional landing place at the Hokianga Heads.
pois.commentary: Tradition holds that the explorer Kupe first landed in Aotearoa at the Hokianga Heads. The reserve sits on the wild, windswept northern head of the Hokianga Harbour entrance. A carved pou marks the spot. For Ngāpuhi and Te Roroa, this is among the most significant wāhi tapu in the country — the beginning point of Māori settlement of New Zealand.

pois.name: Waikare Historic Reserve
pois.short: A dramatically terraced headland pā on the Waikare Inlet with well-preserved earthworks and commanding harbour views.
pois.commentary: This narrow headland on the southern side of the Waikare Inlet at Opua features one of the most visually impressive pā sites in the Bay of Islands. The defensive terracing and ditch-and-bank fortifications are clearly visible. A short walking track leads along the headland.

pois.name: Okuratope Pā
pois.short: One of the best-preserved fortified Māori settlements in Northland — complete terraces, ditches and palisade lines.
pois.commentary: Okuratope is an outstanding example of a complete and well-preserved pā, with clearly visible terracing, defensive ditches, and palisade postholes. Unlike many pā that have been damaged by farming or development, Okuratope retains its full defensive layout, making it invaluable for understanding pre-European Māori military architecture.

pois.name: Moremonui Battlefield
pois.short: Site of the 1807 battle where muskets were first used in Māori warfare — the opening shot of the Musket Wars.
pois.commentary: Near present-day Dargaville, the 1807 Battle of Moremonui was the first known use of muskets in intertribal fighting. Ngāpuhi, armed with muskets acquired from European traders, attacked Ngāti Whātua — but were defeated despite their firepower. The battle set off an arms race that would engulf the entire country in the Musket Wars.

pois.name: Piakoa Burial Caves, Tākou Bay
pois.short: A network of sacred cliff burial caves in Tākou Bay — resting place of significant Ngāpuhi rangatira.
pois.commentary: Piakoa is a network of traditional burial caves in the sea cliffs of Tākou Bay, containing the remains of significant rangatira including Auwha, who led the Ngāpuhi conquest. As a wāhi tapu of the highest order, the caves are tapu and restricted. Heritage New Zealand has listed the site for its outstanding cultural significance.

pois.name: Urupukapuka Island Pā Sites
pois.short: The largest island in the Bay of Islands, covered with pā sites, kainga and complexes of kūmara storage pits.
pois.commentary: Urupukapuka is studded with archaeological sites — multiple pā, kainga (unfortified villages), and extensive kūmara storage pit complexes. Today it's accessible by ferry from Paihia and has excellent walking tracks passing through archaeological sites with interpretation panels.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Urupukapuka Island Pā Sites as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The largest island in the Bay of Islands, covered with pā sites, kainga and complexes of kūmara storage pits. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Te Tii Waitangi Marae
pois.short: The marae directly below the Treaty Grounds — the annual venue for Waitangi Day speeches and debate.
pois.commentary: Te Tii Waitangi Marae sits at the base of the hill below the Treaty Grounds and is the focal point for Waitangi Day commemorations each 6 February. This is where prime ministers and politicians face direct challenge from Māori leaders on the Treaty relationship.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Te Tii Waitangi Marae as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The marae directly below the Treaty Grounds — the annual venue for Waitangi Day speeches and debate. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Kororipo Pā & Stone Store, Kerikeri
pois.short: NZ's oldest standing European buildings — built under the protection of the chief Hongi Hika at his pā.
pois.commentary: Kororipo Pā, overlooking the Kerikeri Basin, was the stronghold of Hongi Hika, the powerful Ngāpuhi chief who facilitated the missionaries' presence in exchange for access to European goods and firearms. The Stone Store (1836) and Mission House (1822) stand nearby as NZ's oldest surviving European buildings.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Kororipo Pā & Stone Store, Kerikeri as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. NZ's oldest standing European buildings — built under the protection of the chief Hongi Hika at his pā. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Te Waimate Mission
pois.short: NZ's second-oldest surviving building — where Darwin visited in 1835 and early Māori-Pākehā exchange took shape.
pois.commentary: Built in 1831–32, Te Waimate Mission House is the second-oldest surviving European building in New Zealand. Charles Darwin visited in December 1835 and was impressed by the Māori agricultural settlement around it.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Te Waimate Mission — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. NZ's second-oldest surviving building — where Darwin visited in 1835 and early Māori-Pākehā exchange took shape. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Matauri Bay — Rainbow Warrior Memorial
pois.short: The final resting place of the Rainbow Warrior — scuttled off Matauri Bay with Māori blessing after the 1985 French bombing.
pois.commentary: After French agents bombed the Greenpeace ship Rainbow Warrior in Auckland Harbour in 1985, killing photographer Fernando Pereira, the refloated ship was scuttled off Matauri Bay in 1987 with the blessing of the local hapū Ngāti Kura. The wreck is now an artificial reef popular with divers.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Matauri Bay — Rainbow Warrior Memorial as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The final resting place of the Rainbow Warrior — scuttled off Matauri Bay with Māori blessing after the 1985 French bombing. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Rawene — Hokianga Heritage
pois.short: A historic Hokianga township with deep Māori roots — timber trade and Treaty-era history on a beautiful harbour.
pois.commentary: Rawene on the Hokianga Harbour has a history stretching back to early Māori settlement. The harbour was one of the most important centres of Māori life in pre-European Northland. The Clendon House overlooks the harbour.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently appreciate the rich historical layers at Rawene — Hokianga Heritage — the site rewards those who take time to read the signage and absorb the context. A historic Hokianga township with deep Māori roots — timber trade and Treaty-era history on a beautiful harbour. Reviewers often describe it as one of the area's quieter but genuinely rewarding stops.

pois.name: Panguru — Birthplace of Dame Whina Cooper
pois.short: A remote Māori community in the hills above the Hokianga — birthplace of Dame Whina Cooper, who led the 1975 Land March.
pois.commentary: Panguru is a small, remote Māori community famous as the birthplace of Dame Whina Cooper, who led the 1975 Māori Land March from Te Hāpua to Parliament. The community maintains strong cultural identity despite its isolation.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Panguru — Birthplace of Dame Whina Cooper as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A remote Māori community in the hills above the Hokianga — birthplace of Dame Whina Cooper, who led the 1975 Land March. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Te Kao — Far North Māori Community
pois.short: A Te Aupouri community in the Far North — one of the most remote Māori settlements, where te reo Māori remains strong.
pois.commentary: Te Kao is one of the northernmost Māori communities in Aotearoa, home to Te Aupouri. The surrounding landscape is rich in archaeological sites and cultural associations.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Te Kao — Far North Māori Community as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A Te Aupouri community in the Far North — one of the most remote Māori settlements, where te reo Māori remains strong. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Whangārei Museum & Heritage Park
pois.short: A heritage park with Māori taonga, colonial buildings, and a kiwi house — telling Northland's layered cultural story.
pois.commentary: Whangārei Museum at Kiwi North combines a museum with significant Māori taonga collections, heritage buildings, and a kiwi house. The museum tells the story of Northland from its earliest Māori settlement through the contact period.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Whangārei Museum & Heritage Park as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A heritage park with Māori taonga, colonial buildings, and a kiwi house — telling Northland's layered cultural story. pois.name: Te Matua Ngahere — Father of the Forest
pois.short: The widest kauri tree in NZ — 16m girth, estimated 2,500–3,000 years old, a living ancestor of Te Roroa.
pois.commentary: Te Matua Ngahere ('Father of the Forest') in Waipoua Forest has the largest girth of any living kauri, wider even than Tāne Mahuta. For Te Roroa, the tree is a tūpuna (ancestor) of immense cultural and spiritual significance. The short walk to the tree passes through primeval kauri forest.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Te Matua Ngahere — Father of the Forest as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. The widest kauri tree in NZ — 16m girth, estimated 2,500–3,000 years old, a living ancestor of Te Roroa. pois.name: Hongi Hika Heritage Trail, Kerikeri
pois.short: A heritage trail through the landscape of Hongi Hika — the Ngāpuhi chief who transformed NZ through the Musket Wars.
pois.commentary: The trail around the Kerikeri Basin connects sites associated with Hongi Hika, one of the most significant figures in Māori history. Hongi visited England in 1820, met King George IV, and used gifts to acquire muskets that gave Ngāpuhi military dominance.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Hongi Hika Heritage Trail, Kerikeri as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. A heritage trail through the landscape of Hongi Hika — the Ngāpuhi chief who transformed NZ through the Musket Wars.
