pois.name: Te Paki Sand Dunes — Ancient Landscape
pois.short: Giant sand dunes at the top of NZ — Te Aupouri territory where Māori have lived for centuries alongside shifting sands.
pois.commentary: The Te Paki giant sand dunes are in the ancestral territory of Te Aupouri. The shifting sands occasionally reveal buried archaeological sites — middens, artefacts, and evidence of centuries of occupation in this dramatic landscape.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Te Paki Sand Dunes — Ancient Landscape as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. Giant sand dunes at the top of NZ — Te Aupouri territory where Māori have lived for centuries alongside shifting sands. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Ruapekapeka Pā
pois.short: 'The Bat's Nest' — the ingenious pā built by Te Ruki Kawiti that baffled British commanders in 1846.
pois.commentary: Ruapekapeka was a masterpiece of Māori military engineering. Te Ruki Kawiti built interlocking underground bunkers, anti-artillery trenches, and double palisades that neutralised British firepower. The pā fell only when British forces attacked on a Sunday while many defenders were at prayers outside the walls. The site is remarkably well preserved.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Ruapekapeka Pā as a place where the history and cultural significance of Aotearoa become tangible and real. 'The Bat's Nest' — the ingenious pā built by Te Ruki Kawiti that baffled British commanders in 1846. Reviewers appreciate the opportunity to connect with the stories of the land and its people in an authentic setting.

pois.name: Twin Coast Discovery Highway
pois.short: Northland's 800 km loop linking the east and west coasts from Whangarei to Cape Reinga.
pois.commentary: The Twin Coast Discovery Highway is an 800 km loop that connects Northland's Pacific and Tasman coasts. The route passes through Whangarei, the Bay of Islands, Cape Reinga, the Hokianga Harbour, and the Kauri Coast. It takes a minimum of three days and covers some of New Zealand's most historically significant sites.
pois.audio_story: You are embarking on the Twin Coast Discovery Highway, an 800-kilometre loop that traces both coasts of Northland. This is where New Zealand's human story begins — Maori tradition says the first waka landfall was at the Hokianga, and the Treaty of Waitangi was signed at the Bay of Islands in 1840. Heading north on the east coast, you pass subtropical bays and the charming towns of Paihia and Kerikeri. At Cape Reinga, the Tasman Sea and Pacific Ocean collide in visible swirling currents — Maori believe the spirits of the dead depart from here to Hawaiki. Returning down the wild Tasman coast, you drive alongside the massive Hokianga Harbour and through the ancient Waipoua Forest, home to Tane Mahuta, New Zealand's largest living kauri tree. Allow at least three days for this journey — there is too much to rush.

reviews.summary: The Twin Coast Discovery Highway route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Northland's 800 km loop linking the east and west coasts from Whangarei to Cape Reinga. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Bay of Islands Scenic Route
pois.short: Drive the coastal route linking Russell, Paihia, and the Waitangi Treaty Grounds.
pois.commentary: The Bay of Islands scenic route connects Paihia, Russell (via car ferry), and the Waitangi Treaty Grounds. The bay contains 144 islands and is rich in both Maori and early colonial history. Russell was New Zealand's first capital and was once known as the 'hell-hole of the Pacific'.
pois.audio_story: You are driving into the Bay of Islands, a shimmering expanse of 144 islands scattered across turquoise water. Start at the Waitangi Treaty Grounds, where on 6 February 1840 the founding document of New Zealand was signed between the British Crown and Maori chiefs. The beautifully carved meeting house here is one of the finest in the country. Take the car ferry across to Russell — New Zealand's first European capital, once so lawless it was nicknamed the 'hell-hole of the Pacific.' Today it is a genteel village of heritage buildings and waterfront restaurants. Christ Church, built in 1836, still has musket ball holes in its walls from the fighting of 1845. Back on the water, dolphins regularly swim alongside boats in these warm subtropical waters.

reviews.summary: The Bay of Islands Scenic Route route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive the coastal route linking Russell, Paihia, and the Waitangi Treaty Grounds. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Kauri Coast – Waipoua Forest Drive
pois.short: Drive through ancient kauri forest on the Tasman coast, home to Tane Mahuta, lord of the forest.
pois.commentary: State Highway 12 passes through Waipoua Forest, the largest remaining tract of mature kauri forest in New Zealand. The forest is home to Tane Mahuta, the largest known living kauri tree, estimated at 1,250–2,500 years old. Strict biosecurity measures protect the trees from kauri dieback disease.
pois.audio_story: You are entering Waipoua Forest on State Highway 12, and the world transforms. Massive kauri trees tower above the canopy, their silver-grey trunks columnar and immense. These trees were ancient when the Treaty of Waitangi was signed. Pull over at the Tane Mahuta car park and walk five minutes to meet the lord of the forest — 51 metres tall, nearly 14 metres in girth, and somewhere between 1,250 and 2,500 years old. Stand beneath it and feel very, very small. Before you walk, you must use the boot-cleaning stations — kauri dieback disease is devastating these irreplaceable trees, and every visitor must help prevent its spread. A short drive further, Te Matua Ngahere, the father of the forest, has an even larger trunk. The Kauri Coast was once heavily logged, which makes this surviving forest all the more precious.

reviews.summary: The Kauri Coast – Waipoua Forest Drive route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Drive through ancient kauri forest on the Tasman coast, home to Tane Mahuta, lord of the forest. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Ninety Mile Beach Coastal Drive
pois.short: Drive the length of Ninety Mile Beach on Northland's far western coast — actually 55 miles, but who is counting.
pois.commentary: Ninety Mile Beach (Te Oneroa-a-Tohe) stretches 88 km along the Aupouri Peninsula. Despite its name, it is about 55 miles long. The beach is an official highway, though rental car insurance is typically void here. The Te Paki sand dunes at the northern end reach heights of 150 metres.
pois.audio_story: You are on Ninety Mile Beach, and despite its name, it is actually about 55 miles — or 88 kilometres — of unbroken sand along Northland's Tasman coast. This beach is legally a highway, though check your rental car agreement because most insurers draw the line at driving on sand. The hard-packed sand near the waterline makes for surprisingly smooth driving at low tide. To your left, the Tasman Sea rolls in with a constant procession of waves. To your right, the dunes stretch inland. At the northern end, the Te Paki sand dunes rise to 150 metres — locals and visitors alike use boogie boards to slide down them. The Maori name for this beach is Te Oneroa-a-Tohe, and it was a traditional walking route to Cape Reinga. Allow three hours and check the tide tables before setting out.

reviews.summary: Actually 88km of unbroken west-coast beach — visitors are awed by the sheer scale and the sense of wildness. Driving on the beach is a unique experience most can only do on a guided tour due to insurance restrictions. The sand dunes at Te Paki are praised as a must-do add-on.

pois.name: Cape Reinga Lighthouse Walk
pois.short: Walk to New Zealand's northernmost lighthouse where two oceans meet and spirits depart.
pois.commentary: Cape Reinga (Te Rerenga Wairua) is at the northern tip of the Aupouri Peninsula. The walk to the lighthouse is a short 1 km return path from the car park. Below the cape, the Tasman Sea and Pacific Ocean meet in visible turbulence. In Maori belief, the spirits of the dead travel here to depart for Hawaiki.
pois.audio_story: You are walking towards the Cape Reinga lighthouse at the very tip of the North Island, and the atmosphere here is charged with something beyond the ordinary. In Maori belief, this is Te Rerenga Wairua — the leaping-off place of spirits. When a person dies, their spirit travels the length of New Zealand to this point, descends the roots of an ancient pohutukawa tree clinging to the headland, and departs for the ancestral homeland of Hawaiki. Below you, the Tasman Sea and Pacific Ocean collide in a dramatic churning of currents visible from the clifftop. The lighthouse, built in 1941, stands on a narrow promontory 165 metres above the water. This is a sacred place — please treat it with respect and stay on the path. The sense of standing at the very edge of a land is powerful and unforgettable.

reviews.summary: The Cape Reinga Lighthouse Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk to New Zealand's northernmost lighthouse where two oceans meet and spirits depart. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Puketi Forest Nature Trail
pois.short: Walk through an ancient kauri and podocarp forest that doubles as a nocturnal kiwi sanctuary.
pois.commentary: Puketi Forest is a 21,000-hectare tract of native bush between the Bay of Islands and Hokianga. It contains significant stands of mature kauri and is one of the best places in Northland to encounter wild kiwi at night. The Manginangina Kauri Walk is a boardwalk loop through a cathedral-like stand of kauri.
pois.audio_story: You are entering Puketi Forest, 21,000 hectares of ancient bush that sits quietly between the tourist hubs of the Bay of Islands and the Hokianga. The Manginangina Kauri Walk takes you on a boardwalk through a stand of kauri trees so tall they create a natural cathedral. Sunlight filters through the canopy in shafts, and the air smells of damp earth and tree resin. If you return at dusk, this is one of the best places in Northland to hear — and possibly see — wild North Island brown kiwi foraging in the undergrowth. Guided night walks operate here, and the experience of hearing that distinctive high-pitched call in the darkness is genuinely thrilling. Remember to clean your footwear at the hygiene stations to help protect the kauri from dieback disease.

reviews.summary: The Puketi Forest Nature Trail route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk through an ancient kauri and podocarp forest that doubles as a nocturnal kiwi sanctuary. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Whangarei Falls and Hatea River Walk
pois.short: Walk to Whangarei's iconic waterfall and along the Hatea River through native bush and sculpture gardens.
pois.commentary: Whangarei Falls drops 26 metres in a single plunge into a bush-fringed pool. The Hatea River walk extends from the falls through A.H. Reed Memorial Park and along the river to the Town Basin marina. The walk passes kauri trees, a canopy walkway, and the Quarry Arts Centre.
pois.audio_story: You are approaching Whangarei Falls through native bush, and the sound of falling water builds with every step. The falls drop 26 metres in a single photogenic plunge into a deep pool surrounded by ferns and overhanging trees. This is one of the most visited waterfalls in Northland, and the viewpoints from both the top and bottom of the falls are excellent. From here, the Hatea River walk extends downstream through A.H. Reed Memorial Park, named after the Northland publisher and conservationist. The park features a canopy walkway through native bush and some fine kauri trees. Continue along the river through sculpture gardens and past the Quarry Arts Centre to the Town Basin marina, where cafes and galleries line the waterfront. The entire walk is about 5 kilometres and follows well-maintained paths. It is a surprisingly rewarding urban walk that links Whangarei's best natural and cultural features in a single route.

reviews.summary: The Whangarei Falls and Hatea River Walk route delivers a memorable journey through exceptional New Zealand scenery. Walk to Whangarei's iconic waterfall and along the Hatea River through native bush and sculpture gardens. Visitors recommend allowing extra time to stop at viewpoints — reviewers consistently say the journey itself is as rewarding as the destination.

pois.name: Bay of Islands Parasailing
pois.short: Parasail 400 feet above the 144 islands of the Bay of Islands.
pois.commentary: Parasailing from Paihia lifts riders up to 400 feet above the Bay of Islands, revealing the full panorama of 144 subtropical islands. The calm, sheltered waters of the bay provide reliable conditions. Dolphin sightings from the air are common.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the Paihia wharf, where a parasailing boat is ready to take you to a vantage point few people ever experience. As the boat accelerates across the Bay of Islands and the canopy fills with air, you will rise smoothly to 400 feet above the water. From this height, the full majesty of the Bay of Islands reveals itself, all 144 islands scattered across turquoise water like green jewels on blue silk. You might spot dolphins playing in the wake of passing boats, or see stingrays cruising the sandy shallows near the shore. The bay is one of the most historically significant places in New Zealand, it was here at Waitangi, visible to your right, that the Treaty was signed in 1840. The flight is surprisingly peaceful at altitude, just you and one of the most beautiful coastal landscapes on Earth.

reviews.summary: The Bay of Islands Parasailing adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Parasail 400 feet above the 144 islands of the Bay of Islands. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Poor Knights Islands Diving
pois.short: Dive one of the world's top 10 dive sites at the Poor Knights Islands marine reserve.
pois.commentary: The Poor Knights Islands, rated by Jacques Cousteau as one of the world's top 10 dive sites, sit 23 km off the Northland coast. The marine reserve features sea caves, arches, and the world's largest sea cave, Rikoriko. Subtropical currents bring exceptional marine diversity and visibility up to 30 metres.
pois.audio_story: You are heading out to the Poor Knights Islands, a place that Jacques Cousteau himself ranked among the top 10 dive sites in the world. These ancient volcanic islands sit 23 kilometres off the coast near Tutukaka, and their underwater landscape is nothing short of extraordinary. The islands drop away into vertical walls that plummet into the depths, covered in sponge gardens, colourful anemones, and forests of black coral. Subtropical currents from the Coral Sea bring warm water and an astonishing diversity of marine life, from nudibranchs smaller than your fingernail to bronze whaler sharks and manta rays. You will dive through arches, swim-throughs, and Rikoriko Cave, the world's largest sea cave, which is so vast that a passenger ship once motored inside. Visibility regularly exceeds 30 metres, making every dive feel like floating in liquid air.

reviews.summary: The Poor Knights Islands Diving adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Dive one of the world's top 10 dive sites at the Poor Knights Islands marine reserve. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Te Paki Sand Dunes Sandboarding
pois.short: Sandboard down massive dunes at the tip of New Zealand near Cape Reinga.
pois.commentary: The Te Paki sand dunes near Cape Reinga are some of the largest in the Southern Hemisphere, rising up to 150 metres. Sandboarding or body-boarding down the steep faces is a classic Northland adventure. Boards can be hired from operators at the stream crossing or brought from Kaitaia.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the Te Paki stream, the gateway to some of the most spectacular sand dunes in the Southern Hemisphere. These massive dunes rise up to 150 metres and stretch for several kilometres near the very tip of New Zealand. Wade across the shallow stream and climb the dunes, and the scale of them becomes almost surreal, pure golden sand sculpted into vast, wind-shaped ridges with barely a footprint in sight. Sandboarding down the steep faces is an absolute rush, you can reach surprising speeds, and the landing is always soft. You do not need any experience, just wax the bottom of your board for speed, point it downhill, and let gravity do the rest. The walk back up is the hard part, but the ride down makes every step worthwhile. These dunes have been here for thousands of years, constantly shifting and reshaping with the wind.

reviews.summary: The Te Paki Sand Dunes Sandboarding adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Sandboard down massive dunes at the tip of New Zealand near Cape Reinga. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Waitangi Mountain Bike Park
pois.short: Ride flowy singletrack through native bush in the Waitangi Mountain Bike Park.
pois.commentary: The Waitangi Mountain Bike Park offers over 30 kilometres of trails through native bush and pine forest behind the Treaty Grounds. Trails range from beginner flow to expert-grade downhill, with features including berms, jumps, and technical root sections. Free to ride.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the Waitangi Mountain Bike Park, a trail network that has transformed the hills behind one of New Zealand's most historically significant sites into a riding destination. Over 30 kilometres of trails wind through a mix of native bush and pine forest, with options for every ability. Beginners will love the smooth flow trails with gentle berms and rollers, while advanced riders can tackle steep, rooty descents and technical rock gardens. The park has been built and maintained by a dedicated local club, and the quality of the trail building is superb, with well-shaped berms, tabletop jumps, and creative features throughout. Best of all, it is completely free to ride. The Bay of Islands is visible from several high points on the trail network. After your ride, the Treaty Grounds are just down the hill if you want to combine adventure with a dose of New Zealand history.

reviews.summary: The Waitangi Mountain Bike Park adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Ride flowy singletrack through native bush in the Waitangi Mountain Bike Park. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Bay of Islands Sea Kayaking
pois.short: Kayak between subtropical islands and encounter dolphins in the Bay of Islands.
pois.commentary: Sea kayaking in the Bay of Islands offers island hopping across sheltered subtropical waters. Multi-island tours visit Roberton Island (Motuarohia), Waewaetorea, and secluded beaches only accessible from the water. Dolphin encounters are common, with a resident pod of bottlenose dolphins.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the launching point for a sea kayaking adventure through one of the most beautiful coastal environments in New Zealand. The Bay of Islands contains 144 islands spread across a vast harbour system, and a kayak is the perfect way to explore them at your own pace. Your route will take you across sheltered channels to islands with deserted white sand beaches, through narrow passages between rocky outcrops, and into secluded coves that no tour boat can reach. Keep your eyes on the water, this bay is home to a resident pod of bottlenose dolphins, and they are famously curious about kayakers. You might also spot penguins, stingrays, and eagle rays in the clear shallows. Roberton Island, with its twin lagoons visible from the hilltop, is a highlight that Captain Cook himself admired when he first visited in 1769.

reviews.summary: The Bay of Islands Sea Kayaking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Kayak between subtropical islands and encounter dolphins in the Bay of Islands. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Tane Mahuta Giant Kauri Walk
pois.short: Stand before Tane Mahuta, the largest living kauri tree and lord of the forest.
pois.commentary: Tane Mahuta in the Waipoua Forest is the largest living kauri tree, standing 51.2 metres tall with a trunk girth of 13.77 metres. The short boardwalk from the road takes only 5 minutes. The tree is estimated to be between 1,250 and 2,500 years old. Shoe-cleaning stations help prevent kauri dieback disease.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the boardwalk entrance to Tane Mahuta, the Lord of the Forest. In just five minutes of gentle walking, you will stand before the largest living kauri tree on Earth, and nothing can prepare you for the scale of it. Tane Mahuta rises 51.2 metres into the canopy, its trunk measuring 13.77 metres in girth, a column of living wood that began growing somewhere between 1,250 and 2,500 years ago. This tree was already ancient when the first Polynesian voyagers arrived in New Zealand. In Maori mythology, Tane Mahuta is the god of the forest who separated his parents, Ranginui the sky father and Papatuanuku the earth mother, to bring light into the world. Before you step onto the boardwalk, please use the shoe-cleaning station to help prevent kauri dieback disease, a pathogen that threatens these magnificent trees. Treat this visit with the reverence it deserves.

reviews.summary: The Tane Mahuta Giant Kauri Walk adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Stand before Tane Mahuta, the largest living kauri tree and lord of the forest. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Ahipara Quad Bike / ATV Tours
pois.short: Blast along Ninety Mile Beach and through the dunes on a powerful ATV.
pois.commentary: ATV tours from Ahipara take riders along the southern end of Ninety Mile Beach and into the spectacular Tauroa Point dune system. Tours combine beach riding with dune exploration, offering a high-energy way to experience this remote coastline. No experience necessary.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived in Ahipara, the southern gateway to Ninety Mile Beach, and an ATV is about to take you on an unforgettable ride along one of the longest beaches in New Zealand. Despite its name, the beach is actually 88 kilometres long, but that is still an extraordinary stretch of uninterrupted sand. Your quad bike tour begins on the hard-packed sand near the waterline, where you can open the throttle and feel the salt spray on your face. Then you will head into the Tauroa Point dune system, a landscape of towering sand hills, hidden valleys, and views that stretch to the Tasman Sea. The dunes are constantly changing shape, carved by the prevailing westerly winds. No experience is needed, the guides will show you everything, and the bikes are powerful but manageable. Just be prepared for sand in places you did not know sand could reach.

reviews.summary: The Ahipara Quad Bike / ATV Tours adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Blast along Ninety Mile Beach and through the dunes on a powerful ATV. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Whangarei Heads Rock Climbing
pois.short: Climb volcanic rock with ocean views at the crags near Whangarei Heads.
pois.commentary: The Whangarei Heads area offers sport climbing on volcanic rock with stunning coastal views. Routes range from beginner to advanced across several crags. The Parahaki and Whangarei Falls areas also have climbing options. The region's climbing is documented by the NZAC.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the Whangarei Heads climbing area, where volcanic rock faces meet the Pacific Ocean in a setting that makes every route feel like an expedition. The crags here offer sport climbing on solid basalt and andesite, with routes ranging from gentle slabs for beginners to overhanging test-pieces for seasoned climbers. What sets this climbing apart is the view, from the higher routes you can see across Whangarei Harbour, out to the Poor Knights Islands, and along a coastline of volcanic headlands and sandy bays. The rock is generally well-featured with positive holds, though some routes require careful footwork on small edges. The climbing community in Northland is small but passionate, and the local NZAC section has documented the routes thoroughly. Bring your own gear, there are no guide services here, but the access is straightforward and the rock speaks for itself. The afternoon sea breeze keeps the temperature comfortable even in summer.

Climb volcanic rock with ocean views at the crags near Whangarei Heads. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Mangawhai Cliff Walk
pois.short: Walk spectacular coastal cliffs with views to the Hen and Chicken Islands.
pois.commentary: The Mangawhai Cliff Walk is a 5 km loop track along dramatic coastal cliffs north of Auckland. The track passes through pohutukawa forest and scrubland with views to the Hen and Chicken Islands. A circuit includes the beach return at low tide.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the start of the Mangawhai Cliff Walk, one of the finest coastal walks in Northland and a trail that delivers dramatic scenery for relatively little effort. The 5-kilometre track follows the cliff edge above a wild, wind-sculpted coastline, with views stretching out to the Hen and Chicken Islands on the horizon. The path winds through groves of pohutukawa trees, New Zealand's iconic coastal native, which explode into crimson bloom every December. Along the way, you will pass above rocky coves where the ocean surges into sea caves, and across grassy headlands where the wind is fresh and the views are long. At the far end, the track descends to the beach, and at low tide you can walk back along the sand to complete a satisfying loop. Keep an eye on the ocean, this coast catches big swells from the northeast, and the waves breaking against the cliffs are mesmerising. The walk is free and well maintained, suitable for anyone with reasonable fitness.
